services.name: IAM
services.description: Manage access to AWS services and resources securely. Create users, groups, roles, and policies to control who can do what.
keyPoints: Users: individual identities with long-term credentials
Groups: collection of users sharing the same permissions
Roles: temporary credentials assumed by services/users (no long-term keys)
Policies: JSON documents defining allow/deny permissions
MFA: add multi-factor authentication for extra security
examTips: Explicit DENY always overrides any ALLOW in IAM policies
Use ROLES instead of access keys whenever possible (EC2 instance profiles, Lambda execution roles)
Policy evaluation: explicit deny > explicit allow > implicit deny
Resource-based policies (S3 bucket policy) vs Identity-based policies (IAM policy)
IAM is GLOBAL — not region-specific

quiz.q: What happens when an IAM policy has both an Allow and a Deny for the same action?
quiz.explanation: In IAM, an explicit Deny ALWAYS overrides an Allow. This is a fundamental IAM evaluation rule.

quiz.q: What should you use instead of IAM user access keys for an EC2 instance that needs S3 access?
quiz.explanation: IAM roles with instance profiles provide temporary, automatically rotated credentials — far more secure than embedding access keys.

services.name: KMS
services.description: Managed service to create and control cryptographic keys used to encrypt your data. Integrated with most AWS services for seamless encryption.
keyPoints: CMKs: Customer Master Keys — symmetric (AES-256) or asymmetric (RSA/ECC)
Key policies: resource-based policies controlling key access
Automatic key rotation: annually for AWS-managed and customer-managed keys
Envelope encryption: encrypt data key with CMK, encrypt data with data key
Integrated with 100+ AWS services (S3, EBS, RDS, etc.)
examTips: AWS-managed keys: AWS creates/manages/rotates. Customer-managed: you control policy, rotation, deletion
KMS keys are REGIONAL — cross-region operations need re-encryption
4 KB limit: KMS can directly encrypt up to 4 KB. For larger data, use envelope encryption
Key deletion has 7-30 day waiting period (cannot be undone after deletion)
CloudTrail logs every KMS API call for auditing

quiz.q: What is the maximum data size KMS can directly encrypt?
quiz.explanation: KMS can directly encrypt up to 4 KB of data. For larger data, use envelope encryption: KMS generates a data key, which encrypts the actual data.

services.name: WAF
services.tagline: Protect web apps from common exploits
services.description: Web application firewall that helps protect web applications from common web exploits that could affect availability, compromise security, or consume excessive resources.
keyPoints: Web ACLs: rules that define allowed/blocked traffic patterns
Managed rule groups: AWS and Marketplace pre-built rules (OWASP Top 10)
Rate-based rules: block IPs exceeding request thresholds
Geo-match: block or allow by country
Deploys on ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway, AppSync
examTips: WAF rules are evaluated in order of priority (lowest number first)
Rate-based rules: minimum threshold is 100 requests per 5 minutes
WAF can inspect: headers, query strings, URI path, body (first 8 KB)
AWS Managed Rules: free core rule set (CRS) for common threats
WAF logs can go to S3, CloudWatch Logs, or Kinesis Data Firehose

quiz.q: Which AWS resources can WAF be deployed on?
quiz.explanation: WAF can be attached to ALB, CloudFront distributions, API Gateway REST APIs, and AppSync GraphQL APIs — not directly on EC2 or S3.

services.name: Shield
services.description: Managed Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) protection service. Shield Standard is automatic and free; Shield Advanced provides enhanced protection and response team access.
keyPoints: Shield Standard: automatic, free, protects against common Layer 3/4 attacks
Shield Advanced: $3,000/month, enhanced protection, DDoS Response Team (DRT)
Advanced: cost protection (AWS credits for scaling costs during attacks)
Advanced: real-time visibility into attacks via CloudWatch
Protects: EC2, ELB, CloudFront, Route 53, Global Accelerator
examTips: Shield Standard is AUTOMATIC and FREE for all AWS customers
Shield Advanced: 24/7 access to DDoS Response Team (DRT)
Advanced provides cost protection — won't charge you for attack-driven scaling
Best DDoS protection combo: CloudFront + Shield Advanced + WAF + Route 53
Shield Advanced has a 1-year commitment

quiz.explanation: AWS Shield Standard is free and automatically enabled for all AWS accounts, protecting against common Layer 3/4 DDoS attacks.

services.name: GuardDuty
services.description: Intelligent threat detection service that continuously monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior using ML, anomaly detection, and integrated threat intelligence.
keyPoints: Analyzes: CloudTrail events, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, EKS audit logs
Uses machine learning and threat intelligence feeds
No agents or software to deploy — enable with one click
Findings: Low, Medium, High severity with detailed remediation
Multi-account support via AWS Organizations
examTips: GuardDuty does NOT prevent attacks — it DETECTS and alerts
Costs based on volume of data analyzed (CloudTrail events, flow logs)
Automate responses: GuardDuty finding → EventBridge → Lambda
Can detect cryptocurrency mining on your instances
30-day free trial, then pay per usage

quiz.q: What data sources does GuardDuty analyze?
options: CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs
quiz.explanation: GuardDuty analyzes CloudTrail management/data events, VPC Flow Logs, DNS query logs, and optionally EKS audit logs and S3 data events.

services.name: Config
services.description: Service that tracks AWS resource configurations and evaluates them against desired settings. Provides configuration history and change notifications.
keyPoints: Configuration recorder: tracks all resource configurations over time
Config Rules: evaluate if resources comply with desired configurations
Managed rules: 300+ pre-built rules (e.g., s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited)
Remediation: auto-fix non-compliant resources via SSM Automation
Aggregator: multi-account/multi-region compliance view
examTips: Config records WHAT changed and WHEN — CloudTrail records WHO made the change
Config rules can trigger automatic remediation via SSM
Conformance packs: pre-built groups of rules for compliance frameworks (PCI, HIPAA)
Config is REGIONAL — use aggregator for multi-region view
Costs per configuration item recorded + per rule evaluation

quiz.q: What is the difference between AWS Config and CloudTrail?
options: Config tracks resource configurations, CloudTrail tracks API calls
CloudTrail tracks configurations, Config tracks API calls
Config is free, CloudTrail is paid
quiz.explanation: AWS Config tracks WHAT your resources look like over time (configuration state). CloudTrail tracks WHO did WHAT (API calls and user activity).

services.name: Cognito
services.description: Add user sign-up, sign-in, and access control to web and mobile apps. Supports social identity providers (Google, Facebook, Apple) and enterprise SAML/OIDC.
keyPoints: User Pools: user directory for sign-up/sign-in (returns JWT tokens)
Identity Pools: provide temporary AWS credentials for resource access
Social sign-in: Google, Facebook, Apple, Amazon
Enterprise federation: SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect
Built-in UI: hosted sign-in page with customizable branding
examTips: User Pools = Authentication (who are you?). Identity Pools = Authorization (what can you access?)
User Pool tokens: ID token (identity), Access token (permissions), Refresh token
Identity Pools can provide unauthenticated (guest) access with limited permissions
ALB can directly integrate with Cognito User Pools for authentication
Lambda triggers: customize flows at sign-up, sign-in, token generation

quiz.q: What is the difference between Cognito User Pools and Identity Pools?
options: User Pools handle sign-in, Identity Pools provide AWS credentials
Identity Pools handle sign-in, User Pools provide AWS credentials
User Pools are for mobile only
quiz.explanation: User Pools handle authentication (sign-up/sign-in, return JWTs). Identity Pools handle authorization (exchange tokens for temporary AWS credentials).
